title: LLM-Based Reranking
concept: Prompting an LLM to score or reorder candidate documents by relevance to the query directly.

exercises.prompt: How did the off-the-shelf cross-encoder models we learned about in the last lesson calculate relevance?

options.text: They feed the query and document together into the model at the same time.
They embed the query and documents separately, then calculate cosine similarity.
They count matching keywords between the query and documents.

exercises.explain: Cross-encoders take a pair of texts (the query and a document) and feed them together into the model to predict a relevance score directly.

cards.title: Why Use a Generative LLM?
cards.body: While cross-encoders are fast, they lack the deep reasoning of large models. By using a generative LLM as your reranker, you can instruct it to look for specific nuances, logic, or tone in your search results.

cards.title: LLM-Based Reranking
cards.body: LLM-Based Reranking uses a generative Large Language Model to evaluate how well a search result matches a query. You feed the query and a document into the prompt and ask the LLM to output a relevance score.

cards.title: The Expert Reviewer
cards.body: Think of using an LLM like having a human expert read through your top pile of books. Instead of just looking at the indexes, they read the pages and tell you exactly which ones answer your question best.

cards.title: Relevance Prompting
cards.body: We prompt the LLM: 'Rate the relevance of this document to the query: "How to fix a leaky pipe". Document: "Plumbing basics: tighten the loose slip nut under the sink." Score from 1-10.' The LLM outputs '9'.

cards.title: The Speed Trap
cards.body: Actually, LLMs are much slower and more expensive than cross-encoders. Use them selectively, only reranking the top 5 to 10 most promising documents to keep your app fast and cost-effective.

cards.title: Cross-Encoder vs. LLM
cards.body: Comparing how classic cross-encoders and generative LLMs handle the reranking step.

left.text: Fast, specialized model that outputs a raw similarity score.

right.text: Slower, but can follow complex rules and reasoning in plain English.

exercises.prompt: What is a major advantage of using an LLM for reranking compared to a fixed cross-encoder?

options.text: It is much faster than using a bi-encoder search.
It requires training a new neural network from scratch.
You can customize the ranking criteria using natural language instructions.
It completely replaces the need for an initial vector search database.

exercises.explain: LLM-based reranking allows you to write natural language instructions (like 'ignore outdated articles'), which is impossible with standard bi-encoders or simple cross-encoders.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You should send all 1,000 search results to the LLM at once for reranking to get the absolute best speed.
exercises.explain: Because LLMs process tokens sequentially and have high latency, passing dozens of documents to an LLM for reranking will make your search feature very slow.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing part to describe where we define the LLM's evaluation rules:
exercises.sentence: We define the scoring rules in the system prompt to guide the LLM's evaluation behavior.
exercises.explain: Setting the persona and scoring instructions in the system prompt ensures the LLM acts as an objective evaluator for the search results.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents an LLM-based reranking implementation?

options.text: import cohere; co = cohere.Client(); co.rerank(query='leaky pipe', documents=docs)
SELECT * FROM documents WHERE content LIKE '%pipe%' ORDER BY date DESC;
System Prompt: 'You are a search grader. Rate the following document's relevance to the user's query from 1 to 5.'

exercises.explain: Option C mimics a realistic LLM system prompt designed to grade document relevance based on a specific query.

exercises.prompt: When prompting an LLM to act as a reranker, which technique is most helpful for reliably sorting the documents in your backend code?

options.text: Request the output in structured JSON format with numeric scores
Ask the LLM to write a free-form essay about each document
Limit the LLM to only outputting 'yes' or 'no' for each document
Count the total number of characters in the LLM's response

exercises.explain: Prompting LLMs to output structured scores directly (like JSON) makes it much easier to parse the numbers programmatically and use them for sorting the documents in your code.
